entries.label: VS Code
entries.description: The most versatile and well-supported development environment available. Microsoft's backing ensures long-term stability, extensive plugin ecosystem, and continuous improvement. Works seamlessly across languages and platforms.

entries.label: AWS CloudFormation
entries.description: Cloud-native IaC is the pragmatic default for single-cloud operations. CloudFormation (including CDK and Bicep for Azure) offers automatic state management, unified security model, 100% resource coverage, direct vendor support, and zero operational overhead. The 2024 IaC Generator eliminates brownfield migration challenges. Choose cloud-native unless you have specific cross-platform orchestration needs or genuine multi-cloud operations.

entries.label: Terraform
entries.description: Solid choice for cross-platform orchestration (AWS + GitHub + Datadog) and genuine multi-cloud operations. State management adds operational overhead, and over 50% of users report state issues. OpenTofu fork addresses licensing concerns. Use when you need multi-tool coordination or have significant existing Terraform investment. For single-cloud infrastructure, cloud-native tools offer better long-term value.

entries.label: N8N
entries.description: Workflow automation platform with extensive integration capabilities. Interesting alternative to building custom async workers for internal processes and lower-volume workloads. Not suitable for high-throughput production systems, but compelling for operational workflows, internal tools, and personal automation.

entries.label: Domain-Driven Design
entries.description: My preferred approach for designing distributed systems. DDD doesn't force monolith vs. microservices decisions prematurely. Bounded contexts let you discover boundaries and scale incrementally. Supports modular monoliths that can split when needed. Requires architectural governance but empowers teams to make detailed design decisions. Most effective for behavior-driven systems rather than pure entity-management scenarios.

entries.label: Test-Driven Development
entries.description: Essential practice for writing maintainable code. TDD encourages SOLID principles, user-focused design, and explicit testing of assumptions. Particularly valuable for developers building architectural discipline, but beneficial for all engineers. Forces consideration of use cases over isolated functions.

entries.label: Trunk-Based Development
entries.description: Critical for effective CI/CD. Enables faster feedback loops, eliminates work based on stale code, and significantly improves system stability. Teams committed to continuous delivery should adopt this practice.

entries.label: REST
entries.description: Still the right choice for entity-driven APIs like media libraries, inventory systems, and configuration management. For business-logic-heavy operations, consider RPC-style APIs instead. Choose based on your domain characteristics, not trends.

entries.label: Behavior Driven Design
entries.description: Not recommended as a modern approach to high-quality development. Industry adoption is low and largely superficial. Most teams using BDD tools like Cucumber or SpecFlow repurpose them for test automation rather than practicing genuine cross-functional behavior specification. The collaboration between business stakeholders and developers that BDD promises rarely materializes, Gherkin scenarios become a maintenance burden at scale, and well-structured TDD with integration tests delivers faster feedback with less ceremony. Shift-left testing and developer experience tooling have largely superseded BDD's value proposition.

entries.label: AWS
entries.description: Primary cloud platform choice based on service longevity and stability. AWS rarely deprecates services, protecting long-term architectural investments. Azure has superior billing management and DevOps tooling, making it attractive for smaller projects. Both are viable, so choose based on organizational priorities and project requirements.

entries.label: Kubernetes
entries.description: Operational complexity and upgrade instability make K8s a difficult commitment. The overhead often outweighs benefits even for large microservice deployments. Carefully evaluate whether managed container services or serverless alternatives better serve your needs before adopting.

entries.label: Firebase
entries.description: Effective for rapid prototyping and initial launches, but limitations emerge quickly in production. Lacks built-in authorization, a major gap that leads to accumulating technical debt. Good for MVPs, but plan migration path early if product gains traction.

entries.label: MySQL
entries.description: Reliable, performant, and universally supported relational database. Works well for service-based architectures that avoid heavy database scripting. PostgreSQL is equally solid. Despite being a .NET developer, I prefer MySQL over SQL Server for most use cases.

entries.label: Redis
entries.description: Avoid based on repeated operational failures across multiple organizations. Stability issues persist even with cloud-managed offerings. Identity crisis (cache? database? message broker?) leads to complex, fragile implementations. Modern cloud-native services solve the same problems more reliably.

entries.label: Memcached
entries.description: Stable, cost-effective caching solution. While clustering has limitations compared to cloud-native options, its simplicity and reliability make it superior to alternatives like Redis when used appropriately. Focus on caching use cases it handles well.

entries.label: DynamoDB
entries.description: Exceptional NoSQL database for the right use cases. Extremely fast, cost-effective, scales seamlessly, integrates perfectly with event-driven architectures. Works beautifully with bounded contexts and can replace entire architectural layers. Native caching support and change-stream capabilities enable real-time data pipelines.

entries.label: Clerk
entries.description: Full-stack authentication and user management platform with excellent UI SDK support. However, the data architecture is deeply flawed, built around single-app use cases with users siloed per application. Cross-app support exists through satellite domains, but this is a workaround for deeper architectural limitations. Great for rapid initial deployment (faster than Firebase or Supabase), but the solution reflects the same deployment-velocity mindset it promotes, lacking long-term architectural planning. Kinde offers similar features at lower cost with better multi-tenant flexibility, multi-domain support, and architectural alignment. While Clerk's SDK advantage is notable, AI-assisted form building eliminates this gap. Consider alternatives that provide better TCO, strategic flexibility, and mature multi-tenant architecture within the same cost range.

entries.label: Kinde
entries.description: Authentication and user management platform that hits the sweet spot among available solutions. Superior cost structure compared to Auth0 and Clerk, with better multi-tenant and branding support than Firebase, Supabase, or AWS Cognito. The data architecture is organization-oriented rather than app-centric, enabling true multi-tenant scenarios without architectural workarounds. Excellent UI console surpasses competing offerings in usability and feature completeness. Simple data migration paths and holistic approach to identity management across multiple applications and domains. While Auth0, Clerk, Firebase, Supabase, AWS Cognito, and WorkOS are all viable solutions for specific use cases, Kinde balances cost, architectural flexibility, enterprise features, and developer experience better than alternatives. Strong consideration for new projects requiring authentication with growth potential.

entries.label: TypeScript
entries.description: Compile-time safety reduces bugs and improves maintainability significantly. Rich tooling and type system help teams understand code better than relying solely on tests. Some verbosity exists for teams new to type safety, but the long-term benefits like fewer runtime errors, better refactoring, and clearer contracts outweigh the learning curve.

entries.label: Vue
entries.description: Lightweight, performant framework with logical separation of concerns. Uses standard HTML without JSX abstraction (unlike React), while remaining flexible and unopinionated (unlike Angular). Smaller ecosystem than competitors but growing rapidly. Best balance of performance, developer experience, and pragmatic design.

entries.label: NUXT
entries.description: Full-stack Vue framework built for projects that need standardized structure and flexible rendering out of the box. Nuxt enforces a standardized project structure with conventions for routing, layouts, middleware, and state management, so teams spend less time debating folder organization and more time building features. The optional server engine (Nitro) provides a unified backend layer for API routes, server middleware, and data fetching without requiring a separate backend project, keeping the full stack in one codebase when that makes sense. Rendering flexibility is a major strength, with SSR, SSG, ISR, SPA, and hybrid rendering modes all available per-route, letting you optimize SEO-critical pages with server rendering while keeping interactive sections client-side. Under the hood, Vue's reactivity system and Nuxt's automatic code splitting, tree shaking, and lazy-loaded components deliver fast load times and smooth runtime performance. Auto-imports for composables, components, and utilities reduce boilerplate significantly. Version upgrades still require attention, and some abstractions over libraries like Tailwind can be limiting, but for projects that benefit from SSR, SEO, or a unified full-stack TypeScript experience, Nuxt delivers strong velocity with minimal configuration.

entries.label: GRPC
entries.description: High-performance RPC framework built on HTTP/2 with Protocol Buffers for strongly typed service contracts. gRPC excels at internal service-to-service communication where low latency and efficient binary serialization matter more than human-readable payloads. Contract-first development enforces clear API boundaries between services, and code generation from .proto files eliminates the drift between client and server that plagues REST-based integrations over time. Bidirectional streaming support opens patterns that REST simply cannot match, such as real-time feeds and long-lived connections. Tight coupling is a legitimate concern, but proper bounded context design keeps it manageable. Unlike WCF, gRPC is built on open standards with broad cross-language support, so the coupling is in the contract, not the platform. The .NET implementation is first-class, with native integration into ASP.NET and excellent tooling in Aspire for local development.

entries.label: GraphQL
entries.description: Effective for internal tools and reporting dashboards. However, production experience revealed it often masks architectural and organizational problems rather than solving them. Modern team topologies, such as vertical slices and domain ownership, eliminate the API bottleneck GraphQL was meant to solve. Security, performance, and maintenance tradeoffs are significant. Use selectively.

entries.label: .NET
entries.description: Premier platform for enterprise systems. Mature, secure, well-supported ecosystem with excellent performance. While other languages may be simpler or have cleaner evolution paths, .NET strikes the best balance between technical capability and business velocity. Continuous innovation keeps it ahead of alternatives like Java.

entries.label: Jekyll
entries.description: Rock-solid static site generator backed by GitHub Pages. Perfect for content-heavy sites like blogs, documentation, and portfolios where dynamic features aren't needed. A simple architecture built on Liquid templates, YAML front matter, and Markdown content keeps it maintainable without framework churn. Native GitHub Pages integration means zero-config deployment. Compile-time rendering ensures exceptional performance and security. Great for technical writing where you want version control and simplicity over CMS complexity.

entries.label: .NET Aspire
entries.description: Should be the default starting point for any ASP.NET solution. The single biggest reason to adopt Aspire is the ability to test a fully distributed system on your local machine without provisioning remote environments. No shared dev clusters, no waiting on cloud deployments to validate integration behavior. You spin up the entire topology locally, including databases, caches, and message brokers, and test service interactions end-to-end before code ever leaves your machine. Even if you never deploy Aspire to production, this alone justifies adoption. Aspire orchestrates everything with a single F5 keystroke, eliminating Docker Compose files and manual startup scripts. The built-in dashboard gives you structured logging, distributed tracing, and metrics across all services out of the box, turning local debugging from guesswork into observability. Service discovery and connection string management are automatic, so adding a new dependency is a one-liner in your AppHost rather than configuration scattered across projects. Health checks, retry policies, and OpenTelemetry come wired in through opinionated defaults that follow production best practices. New team members spin up the full system in minutes instead of hours. For production, Aspire generates deployment manifests for Azure Container Apps, Kubernetes, or custom targets, but the lasting payoff is making distributed .NET development feel as simple as running a single project.

entries.label: WinUI 3
entries.description: The default choice for new Windows desktop and tablet applications. WinUI 3 is the successor to UWP, which Microsoft has effectively abandoned, and ships as part of the Windows App SDK with full access to Win32 APIs, MSIX packaging, and modern XAML rendering. Applications that require elevated privileges, deep OS integration, strict compliance and regulatory controls, or native hardware access should start here. WPF remains a reasonable choice for maintaining and extending legacy applications given its mature ecosystem, extensive community tooling, and continued Microsoft support, but new projects benefit from WinUI 3's modern control library, better performance on current hardware, and alignment with Microsoft's forward investment. PWAs cover the middle ground for software distribution where moderate machine interop is sufficient, but they fall short for scenarios demanding admin-level system access, offline-first reliability with local data, or auditability requirements common in regulated industries. WinUI 3 pairs naturally with .NET Aspire for service integration and MVVM Toolkit for clean architecture patterns.

entries.label: .NET Blazor
entries.description: Blazor attempted to solve a problem that didn't need solving: letting C# developers avoid JavaScript. The premise sounds appealing until you ask what it actually delivers beyond established, better-supported web stacks. Blazor Server's reliance on persistent SignalR connections is an architectural liability, not a feature. Every user holds a WebSocket, requires server affinity, and consumes server-side memory for circuit state. That model is at odds with how production web applications need to scale. WebAssembly mode ships an entire .NET runtime to the browser, and if you genuinely need WASM, you don't need Blazor to get there. The self-contained application model is tech debt the moment it's written. It couples your UI to a runtime in ways that resist incremental modernization, independent scaling, and access to the full web ecosystem. Even as a POC tool, Blazor builds on a foundation that leads nowhere production-worthy. You're prototyping on a stack you'll abandon. A decoupled architecture with a proper frontend framework like Vue or Nuxt and .NET APIs on the backend provides better separation of concerns, broader ecosystem support, and a path that actually scales. Blazor is not without merit in extremely narrow internal tooling scenarios, but for anyone building products with paying customers and production stakes, it creates more problems than it solves.
